steps.title: Identify what the names do in the sentence
steps.content:
The sentence describes the student discovering biographical sketches of two other Black Americans who served in Congress.

Then it names those two specific people: Harold Washington of Illinois and Augustus (Gus) Freeman Hawkins of California.

The names serve as a list that identifies who those two people are — they elaborate on or exemplify the category mentioned just before them.

steps.title: Match the punctuation rule for introducing lists and elaborations
steps.content:
A colon is used to introduce a list, example, or elaboration that follows.

Here, it marks the shift from the general group to the specific names that make up that group.

A period would incorrectly break the sentence into a fragment (the second part has no verb); a semicolon would wrongly treat the names as a second independent clause; no punctuation leaves the connection grammatically incomplete and awkward. That matches choice D — D is correct.
